elements.sent-2411-001: Multi-modal Large Language models (MLLMs) are always trained on data from diverse and unreliable sources, which may contain misaligned or mislabeled text-image pairs.
elements.sent-2411-002: This frequently causes robustness issues and hallucinations, leading to performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-003: Data valuation is an efficient way to detect and trace these misalignments.
elements.sent-2411-004: Nevertheless, existing methods are computationally expensive for MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-005: While computationally efficient, the classical influence functions are inadequate for contrastive learning models because they were originally designed for pointwise loss.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, contrastive learning involves minimizing the distance between the modalities of positive samples and maximizing the distance between the modalities of negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-007: This requires us to evaluate the influence of samples from both perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-008: To tackle these challenges, we introduce the Extended Influence Function for Contrastive Loss (ECIF), an influence function crafted for contrastive loss.
elements.sent-2411-009: ECIF considers both positive and negative samples and provides a closed-form approximation of contrastive learning models, eliminating the need for retraining.
elements.sent-2411-010: Building upon ECIF, we develop a series of algorithms for data evaluation in MLLM, misalignment detection, and misprediction trace-back tasks.
elements.sent-2411-011: Experimental results demonstrate our ECIF advances the transparency and interpretability of MLLMs by offering a more accurate assessment of data impact and model alignment compared to traditional baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-012: Multi-modal Large Language models (MLLMs) \citep{yin2023survey,koh2024generating have garnered significant attention for their ability to integrate and understand various data types, such as image, text, and audio.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite their growing application, existing MLLMs often suffer from robustness issues \citep{carlini2021poisoning and hallucinations, primarily stemming from misaligned text-image pairs in the training data~\citep{Kim_2023_ICCV .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: These misalignments, manifesting as semantic mismatches, contextual inconsistencies, or discrepancies between abstract and concrete elements, can severely degrade model performance.
elements.sent-2411-015: MLLMs assume consistent alignment between image-text pairs, but when this assumption fails, it leads to incorrect interpretations, ultimately degrading model performance.
elements.sent-2411-016: Consequently, improving dataset transparency is crucial, as model developers need the ability to trace and identify problematic data samples.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, diagnosing issues caused by misaligned data, such as mislabeled or biased samples, is difficult when working with large text-image datasets.
elements.sent-2411-018: Although the critical role of training data in shaping MLLM capabilities is well recognized, there remains a lack of robust evaluation mechanisms for data quality~\citep{NEURIPS2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: To address this, various data valuation methods~\citep{jia2019towards,ghorbani2019data,yoon2020data,han2020explaining have been introduced to enhance dataset transparency by quantifying the contribution of individual data points to model performance.}
elements.sent-2411-020: These approaches typically assign higher contribution scores to training instances whose inclusion significantly boosts model performance compared to their exclusion.
elements.sent-2411-021: Some methods, such as Shapley Value~\citep{pmlr-v151-kwon22a , require multiple retraining processes with different subsets of data, which is computationally expensive and impractical for large models.}
elements.sent-2411-022: To overcome this limitation, influence function-based methods have gained popularity, as they estimate data contributions using gradient information, thereby avoiding retraining~\citep{choe2024your .}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, applying influence functions to MLLMs poses significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, MLLMs rely on noise-contrastive estimation~\citep{radford2021learning,gutmann2010noise,he2020momentum as their training objective.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This objective encourages the model to draw positive pairs closer in feature space while pushing negative pairs apart, making the influence function unsuitable for direct application to contrastive loss.
elements.sent-2411-026: However, the original definition of the influence function does not consider the roles of positive and negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-027: This oversimplified analysis is particularly prone to underestimating the impact of certain hard negative samples on the learning process~\citep{chen2020multi .}
elements.sent-2411-028: To address these challenges, we propose the Extended Influence Function for Contrastive Loss (ECIF), a novel method designed to quantify data importance specifically for contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-029: ECIF enjoys a closed-form approximation of the original contrastive loss, thus eliminating the need for re-training - a process that is impractical in the era of large models.
elements.sent-2411-030: It also accounts for the dual role of data points as both positive and negative samples, providing a more comprehensive understanding of their impact on model training.
elements.sent-2411-031: This approach provides a more accurate measurement of misalignment.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We propose ECIF, the first dual-perspective data valuation method for MLLMs, which quantifies the impact of data points as both positive and negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-034: This comprehensive approach enables a more accurate measurement of data contribution, particularly addressing the influence of negative samples in contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-035: Based on ECIF, we develop corresponding algorithms for different tasks, including identifying the most valuable data (related to specific tasks), misalignment detection, and misprediction trace-back.
elements.sent-2411-036: Comprehensive experimental results demonstrate that ECIF can effectively and efficiently remove the influence of samples compared to retraining and identify influential data in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-037: Moreover, our methods based on ECIF are also effective in identifying influential data (harmful data and valuable data) for fine-tuning, mispredictions trace back, and detecting misaligned data.
elements.sent-2411-038: Recently, self-supervised contrastive learning \citep{pmlr-v119-chen20j has emerged as a highly effective approach for acquiring representations without the need for labeled data \citep{donahue2019large .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: This model utilizes a contrastive loss, which pushes dissimilar data pairs apart while pulling similar pairs closer together.
elements.sent-2411-040: Contrastive learning plays a pivotal role in advancing MLLMs by integrating and understanding information across diverse modalities, such as text and images \citep{radford2021learning,jiang2024hallucination .}
elements.sent-2411-041: In multi-modal contrastive learning tasks, proper alignment of the training data ensures accurate cross-modal associations, enabling models to learn and extract consistent feature representations \citep{wang2020understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-042: One of the key challenges in training with noisy, large-scale image-text pairs sourced from the internet is achieving effective alignment between these modalities.
elements.sent-2411-043: To address this, researchers have developed various methods, such as those proposed by \cite{gao2022pyramidclip and \cite{yao2021filip , which introduce finer-grained and more extensive interactions between text and images to improve cross-modal alignment.}}

elements.sent-2411-045: Our work bridges this gap by applying influence functions in contrastive learning, allowing for a deeper understanding of both positive and negative samples.

elements.sent-2411-219: In this paper, we introduced the Extended Influence Function for Contrastive Loss (ECIF), a novel method to quantify data valuation in MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-220: ECIF provides a dual-perspective analysis of data points by considering both positive and negative samples, offering a more comprehensive understanding of their impact on model performance.
elements.sent-2411-221: By utilizing a closed-form approximation, ECIF eliminates the need for re-training, making it highly practical for large models.
elements.sent-2411-222: Our approach is applicable to enhancing fine-tuning, tracing mispredicted data, and detecting misaligned data, with results demonstrating its effectiveness in real-world tasks.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Task-related Influence Score Based on ECIF\label{alg:task-related}} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE {\bf Input:} Training Dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{ (x^T, x^I) \}$, dataset $\mathcal{D}^*$ to be evaluated, test dataset $\mathcal{D}'$, the parameters $\hat{\theta}$ which is involved in IF calculation in the model. \STATE Compute the $\text{ECIF}(\mathcal{D}^*, \mathcal{D})= \left(\bar{\text{positive-IF}}, \bar{\text{negative-IF}}\right)$ by algorithm \ref{alg:ECIF}. \STATE Compute the gradient of the batch loss function of the test data as \begin{equation}C = \sum_{(U,V)\in D'}\nabla_{\theta} L_{Batch}(U, V;\hat{\theta})\end{equation} \STATE Compute the task-related influence score as \begin{equation*} IS = C^{\mathrm{T}}\cdot{\text{positive-IF}} + C^{\mathrm{T}}\cdot{\text{negative-IF}} \end{equation*} \STATE {\bf Return: } Task-related Influence Score $IS$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Self Influence Score Based on ECIF\label{alg:task-related-self}} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE {\bf Input:} Training Dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{ (x^T, x^I) \}$, dataset $\mathcal{D}^*$ to be evaluated, test dataset $\mathcal{D}'$, the parameters $\hat{\theta}$ which is involved in IF calculation in the model. \STATE Compute the $\text{ECIF}(\mathcal{D}^*, \mathcal{D})= \left(\bar{\text{positive-IF}}, \bar{\text{negative-IF}}\right)$ by algorithm \ref{alg:ECIF}. \STATE Compute the gradient of the batch loss function of the test data as \begin{equation}C = \frac{1}{|D'|}\sum_{(x^T,x^I)\in D'}\nabla_{\theta} -\log \frac{u^{T}\cdot v}{\|u\|\cdot\|v\|},\end{equation} where $u$ and $v$ is the embedding for $x^T$ and $x^I$ \STATE Compute the task-related influence score as \begin{equation*} IS = C^{\mathrm{T}}\cdot{\text{positive-IF}} + C^{\mathrm{T}}\cdot{\text{negative-IF}} \end{equation*} \STATE {\bf Return: } Task-related Influence Score $IS$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Relative Influence Score Based on ECIF\label{alg:relative-IS}} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE {\bf Input:} Training Dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{ (x^T, x^I) \}$, dataset $\mathcal{D}^*$ to be evaluated, test dataset $\mathcal{D}'$. \STATE Compute the $\text{ECIF}(\mathcal{D}^*, \mathcal{D}) = \left({\text{positive-IF}}, {\text{negative-IF}}\right)$ by algorithm \ref{alg:ECIF}. \STATE Compute the gradient of the batch loss function of the test data as \begin{equation}C = \sum_{(U,V)\in D'}\nabla_{\theta} L_{Batch}(U, V;\hat{\theta})\end{equation} \IF{${\text{positive-IF}}$ is parallel to ${\text{negative-IF}}$} \STATE Compute the relative-IS as \begin{equation*} \text{relative-IS} ={\|{\text{positive-IF}}\|}^{-1}\left|C^{\mathrm{T}} {\text{negative-IF}} \right| \end{equation*} \ELSE[${\text{positive-IF}}$ is not parallel to ${\text{negative-IF}}$] \STATE Define $I=\left[{\text{positive-IF}}, {\text{negative-IF}}\right]$. \STATE Compute the relative-IS as \begin{equation*} \text{relative-IS} = {\|C\|}^{-1} \left|C^{\mathrm{T}} I \left[I^{\mathrm{T}}\cdot I\right]^{-1} I^{\mathrm{T}}C\right| \end{equation*} \ENDIF \STATE {\bf Return:} $\text{relative-IS}$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Relative Influence Score Based on ECIF\label{alg:relative-IS-self}} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE {\bf Input:} Training Dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{ (x^T, x^I) \}$, dataset $\mathcal{D}^*$ to be evaluated, test dataset $\mathcal{D}'$. \STATE Compute the $\text{ECIF}(\mathcal{D}^*, \mathcal{D}) = \left({\text{positive-IF}}, {\text{negative-IF}}\right)$ by algorithm \ref{alg:ECIF}. \STATE Compute the gradient of the batch loss function of the test data as \begin{equation}C = \frac{1}{|D'|}\sum_{(x^T,x^I)\in D'}\nabla_{\theta} -\log \frac{u^{T}\cdot v}{\|u\|\cdot\|v\|},\end{equation} where $u$ and $v$ is the embedding for $x^T$ and $x^I$ \IF{${\text{positive-IF}}$ is parallel to ${\text{negative-IF}}$} \STATE Compute the relative-IS as \begin{equation*} \text{relative-IS} ={\|{\text{positive-IF}}\|}^{-1}\left|C^{\mathrm{T}} {\text{negative-IF}} \right| \end{equation*} \ELSE[${\text{positive-IF}}$ is not parallel to ${\text{negative-IF}}$] \STATE Define $I=\left[{\text{positive-IF}}, {\text{negative-IF}}\right]$. \STATE Compute the relative-IS as \begin{equation*} \text{relative-IS} = {\|C\|}^{-1} \left|C^{\mathrm{T}} I \left[I^{\mathrm{T}}\cdot I\right]^{-1} I^{\mathrm{T}}C\right| \end{equation*} \ENDIF \STATE {\bf Return:} $\text{relative-IS}$. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-305: We employ three datasets for our utility and efficiency evaluation tasks, as well as for the misprediction traceback experiments: FGVC-Aircraft dataset~\citep{maji2013finegrainedvisualclassificationaircraft , Food101 dataset~\citep{bossard2014food , Flowers102 dataset~\citep{4756141 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-306: The FGVC-Aircraft dataset comprises 10,000 images of airplanes, each annotated with the model and bounding box of the dominant aircraft depicted.
elements.sent-2411-307: The Food-101 dataset, publicly available for food image recognition, includes 101 food categories, with each category containing 1,000 images.
elements.sent-2411-308: The images feature food photographs captured from various angles and under different lighting conditions.
elements.sent-2411-309: The Flowers-102 dataset consists of 102 classes of flowers native to the United Kingdom, with each class containing between 40 and 258 images.
elements.sent-2411-310: We use Cifar-10 dataset~\citep{Krizhevsky2009LearningML for the misalignment detection tasks.. {\bf Implementation Details.}}
elements.sent-2411-311: Our experiments utilized an Nvidia V100-32G GPU and 10 CPU cores with 64 GB memory.
elements.sent-2411-312: For all the following tasks, we employ the CLIP model 'ViT-B/16' and use LoRA few-shot learning.
elements.sent-2411-313: For the experiment of Identifying influential data for fine-tuning, we first calculate the task-related IS for every individual sample and collect valuable data with positive IS, then choose to remove $0$-$30\%$ of these.
elements.sent-2411-314: We conduct the experiments $3$ times for each removal with different seeds.
elements.sent-2411-315: The experiment setting for harmful data removal is similar.
elements.sent-2411-316: Differently, we select harmful data with negative IS.
elements.sent-2411-317: The experiments are conducted on Food101, Flowers102, FGVC-Aircraft, and DTD datasets, and the remove ratio ranges $0$ to $90\%$.
elements.sent-2411-318: For each removal, we conducted the experiments 3 times with different seeds.
elements.sent-2411-319: The multiple samples removal experiments are conducted on Food101, Flowers102, FGVC-Aircraft, and DTD datasets, with removal ratios from $1\%$ to $7\%$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-320: For the misprediction trace back task, we conduct experiments on Food101, Flowers102, FGVC-Aircraft, and DTD datasets.
elements.sent-2411-321: We first choose a mispredicted test sample as the target in algorithm \ref{alg:task-related-self , then calculate the relative IS for each individual sample in the training dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-322: Noting the relative IS is always positive.
elements.sent-2411-323: We visualize training samples with top-$10$ relative IS.
elements.sent-2411-324: For the misalignment detection tasks, Cifar-10 and imagenette (smaller version of imageNet) datasets are used.
elements.sent-2411-325: We also applied standard data augmentation techniques on the training set,i.e., random cropping and random flipping.
elements.sent-2411-326: The model is optimized with Adam with weight decay ($5e-1$), and $\beta$ is set to $0.9$.
elements.sent-2411-327: A dropout ratio of $0.25$ is used.
elements.sent-2411-328: The training iterations are set to $30$, with a learning rate of $2e-4$ and a batch size of $16$.
elements.sent-2411-329: The rank of the low-rank matrices of LoRA is set to $2$.
elements.sent-2411-330: We trained the model on a poisoned version of the dataset ($20\%$ / $30\%$ of the data samples are mislabeled).
elements.sent-2411-331: Then, we compute the influence score IS of all the training samples on the mispredicted test samples.
elements.sent-2411-332: At the end, we visualize the training samples that have the highest positive IS score.
elements.sent-2411-333: To comprehensively evaluate the data removal capabilities of ECIF in various scenarios, we conducted experiments on the performance when multiple samples need to be removed.
elements.sent-2411-334: Specifically, we consider the different ratios of samples ($1$-$7\%$) for removal.
elements.sent-2411-335: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:hyperpara , we can see the accuracy difference between these two methods is very small (less than 1.5\%) in most cases, except the case of $2\%$ for Food101.}
elements.sent-2411-336: These results show the utility of ECIF compared to the ground truth.

elements.sent-2411-338: Thus, ECIF is an editing method that achieves a trade-off between speed and effectiveness.

elements.sent-2411-342: Contrastive learning, commonly applied in large-scale multimodal models, often relies on data from diverse and often unreliable sources, which can include misaligned or mislabeled text-image pairs.
elements.sent-2411-343: This frequently leads to robustness issues and hallucinations, ultimately causing performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-344: Nevertheless, existing methods are computationally expensive for large-scale models.
elements.sent-2411-345: Although computationally efficient, classical influence functions are inadequate for contrastive learning models, as they were initially designed for pointwise loss.
elements.sent-2411-346: Furthermore, contrastive learning involves minimizing the distance between positive sample modalities while maximizing the distance between negative sample modalities.
elements.sent-2411-347: This necessitates evaluating the influence of samples from both perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-348: Building upon ECIF, we develop a series of algorithms for data evaluation, misalignment detection, and misprediction trace-back tasks.
elements.sent-2411-349: Experimental results demonstrate our ECIF advances the transparency and interpretability of CLIP-style embedding models by offering a more accurate assessment of data impact and model alignment compared to traditional baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-350: Contrastive learning has become a cornerstone in the development of multimodal models due to its ability to align representations from different modalities—such as images, text, and audio—within a shared semantic space~\citep{chen2020simpleframeworkcontrastivelearning, yin2023survey,koh2024generating .}
elements.sent-2411-351: However, models trained with contrastive learning often suffer from robustness issues~\citep{carlini2021poisoning and hallucinations, which are primarily attributed to misaligned text-image pairs in the training data~\citep{Kim_2023_ICCV .}}
elements.sent-2411-352: Contrastive learning relies on the assumption of consistent alignment between image-text pairs; however, when this assumption fails, it leads to incorrect interpretations, ultimately degrading model performance.
elements.sent-2411-353: Although the critical role of training data in shaping the capabilities of multimodal models is well recognized, robust evaluation mechanisms for data quality remain lacking~\citep{NEURIPS2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-354: However, applying influence functions to models trained with contrastive learning presents significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-355: However, multimodal models rely on noise-contrastive estimation~\citep{radford2021learning,gutmann2010noise,he2020momentum as their training objective.}
elements.sent-2411-356: To address these challenges, we propose the Extended Influence Function for Contrastive Loss (ECIF), a novel method designed to quantify data attribution specifically for contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-357: We propose ECIF, the first dual-perspective data valuation method for contrastive learning, which quantifies the impact of data points as both positive and negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-358: Moreover, our methods based on ECIF are also effective in identifying influential data (harmful data and valuable data) for fine-tuning, misprediction trace-back, and detecting misaligned data.
elements.sent-2411-359: Contrastive learning plays a pivotal role in advancing multimodal models by integrating and understanding information across diverse modalities, such as text and images \citep{radford2021learning,jiang2024hallucination .}
elements.sent-2411-360: To address this, researchers have developed various methods, such as those proposed by \citet{gao2022pyramidclip and \citet{yao2021filip , which introduce finer-grained and more extensive interactions between text and images to improve cross-modal alignment.}}
elements.sent-2411-361: Influence function, initially a staple in robust statistics \citep{cook2000detection,cook1980characterizations , has seen extensive adoption within deep learning since \citep{koh2017understanding .}}
elements.sent-2411-362: Recent advancements, such as the LiSSA method \citep{agarwal2017second,kwon2023datainf, grosse2023studying and kNN-based techniques \citep{guo2021fastif , have been proposed to enhance the computational efficiency of computing the influence function.}}

elements.sent-2411-399: In our experiments, we will apply our above methods to different tasks, including identifying influential data (harmful data and valuable data) for fine-tuning through the task-related influence score, mispredictions trace-back, and detecting misaligned data.
elements.sent-2411-400: For misalignment detection tasks, we use Cifar-10dataset~\citep{Krizhevsky2009Learning , and Imagenette, a smaller subset of 10 easily classified classes from Imagenet~\citep{5206848 .}}
elements.sent-2411-401: The tasks described below directly implement the algorithms for the applications in the previous section.
elements.paragraph-2411-010: Ground Truth, Baselines and Evaluation Metric.
elements.sent-2411-402: We use retraining as the ground truth, where the CLIP model is fine-tuned from scratch after sample removal.
elements.sent-2411-403: Additionally, we adopt ECIF along with three other methods as baselines (see Appendix \ref{app:baseline ).}
elements.sent-2411-404: We utilize two main evaluation metrics to assess our models: accuracy and runtime (RT)\footnote{Here, RT refers to the total runtime required for the updating process.. .}
elements.sent-2411-405: Runtime, measured in minutes, assesses the time required for each method to update the model.
elements.sent-2411-406: We can easily observe that with random data removal, ECIF achieves an accuracy nearly equivalent to retraining (84.87 compared to 84.93) while significantly reducing runtime from 291.80 minutes to 29.12 minutes on the Food101 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-407: A similar trend was observed in the Flowers102 dataset, where ECIF reduces runtime from 331.80 minutes for retraining to 29.16 minutes, along with a modest 0.37 point improvement in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-408: These findings demonstrate the ability of ECIF to save approximately 40-50\% of the computational time required for retraining while maintaining comparable accuracy levels.
elements.sent-2411-409: When valuable data identified by ECIF are removed, the accuracy of both the retrained model and ECIF's edited version closely align, and both are significantly lower than those observed with random removal.

elements.sent-2411-412: In this paper, we introduced the Extended Influence Function for Contrastive Loss (ECIF), a novel method for data valuation in contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-413: ECIF provides a dual-perspective analysis of data by considering both positive and negative samples, offering a comprehensive understanding of their impact on model performance.
elements.sent-2411-414: Utilizing a closed-form approximation, ECIF bypasses the need for retraining, making it both efficient and scalable for large models.
elements.sent-2411-415: Our approach is applicable to enhancing fine-tuning, tracing mispredicted data, and detecting misaligned data, with results demonstrating its effectiveness in real-world tasks.
elements.sent-2411-416: This research advances the methodologies for data attribution in contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-417: While we recognize the significance of assessing societal impacts, our analysis indicates that there are no immediate ethical risks that necessitate specific mitigation measures beyond standard practices in machine learning.. \bibliographystyle{icml2025 \onecolumn.}
elements.sent-2411-418: Consider the dataset $\mathcal{D ^*$, define \begin{equation}L'(\mathcal{D}^*, \text{Seg}; \theta)\triangleq\text{Pos}(\mathcal{D}^*, \text{Seg}; \theta) + \cdot\text{Neg}(\mathcal{D}^*, \text{Seg}; \theta),\end{equation} Then the loss function with the influence of $\mathcal{D ^*$ removed becomes \begin{equation}\label{eq:approxiated_loss} \begin{split} L^-(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{D}^*, \text{Seg}; \theta) = L_{Total}(\mathcal{B}; \theta) - L'(\mathcal{D}^*, \text{Seg}; \theta). \end{split}\end{equation} Equation \eqref{eq:approxiated_loss is based on the assumption that the influence of data acting as positive and negative samples on model parameters can be linearly superimposed, and we can leverage ECIF to edit the model based on the following corollary.}}}

elements.subsection-2411-021: Extending Utility and Efficiency Evaluation to Larger Dataset
elements.sent-2411-428: We use Cifar-100 dataset~\citep{krizhevsky2009learningML for our utility and efficiency evaluation tasks.}
elements.figure-2411-019: \caption{Comparison of different removal and update strategies on CIFAR-100.} \label{tab:cifar100} \begin{tabular}{lccc} Removal Type Method Accuracy (\%) Time (s) Random Retrain $73.50 \pm 0.35$ $12.56 \pm 0.37$ IF Update $73.00 \pm 0.20$ $7.40 \pm 0.11$ Positive Retrain $73.50 \pm 0.41$ $8.02 \pm 0.15$ IF Update $72.92 \pm 0.31$ $2.70 \pm 0.17$ Negative Retrain $72.83 \pm 0.12$ $7.92 \pm 0.01$ IF Update $73.00 \pm 0.20$ $2.26 \pm 0.19$ \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-429: Results in table 4 \ref{tab:cifar100 highlight the performance of various removal and update strategies on CIFAR-100.}
elements.sent-2411-430: The results demonstrate the effectiveness of IF Update (influence function) compared to traditional retraining in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-431: Across all removal types (Random, Positive, and Negative), IF Update consistently achieves comparable or higher accuracy while significantly reducing runtime.
elements.sent-2411-432: For Random data removal, IF Update improves accuracy from 66.67\% $\pm$ 2.36\% (Retrain) to 73.33\% $\pm$ 1.18\% and nearly halves the runtime, decreasing from 6.87 ± 0.19 seconds to 3.85 ± 0.13 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-433: Similarly, under Positive removal, IF Update achieves an accuracy boost from 68.33\% $\pm$ 1.18\% (Retrain) to 72.50\% $\pm$ 2.04\%, with runtime reduced from 3.01 ± 0.05 seconds to 0.97 $\pm$ 0.09 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-434: Lastly, for Negative removal, while the retrained model yields a slightly higher accuracy (73.33\% $\pm$ 2.36\%) compared to IF Update (70.83\% $\pm$ 1.18\%), IF Update achieves comparable performance with a runtime of 3.49 $\pm$ 4.16 seconds, closely matching retraining (3.00 $\pm$ 0.04 seconds).
elements.sent-2411-435: These results validate the utility of IF Update as a computationally efficient alternative to retraining, achieving near-equivalent or superior accuracy across varied removal scenarios on CIFAR-100.
elements.subsection-2411-022: Extending Harmful Data Removal to Real-World Noisy Dataset
elements.sent-2411-436: We use ANIMAL-10N dataset~\citep{song2019selfie for our harmful data removal tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-437: It's a real world noisy dataset, containing five pairs of "confusing" animals: {(cat, lynx), (jaguar, cheetah), (wolf, coyote), (chimpanzee, orangutan), (hamster, guinea pig) , where two animals in each pair look very similar.}
elements.sent-2411-438: Overall, the proportion of incorrect labels was 6.44\%.
elements.sent-2411-439: The harmful removal task on ANIMAL-10N is presented in table \ref{exp:noisy_removal .We observe that when a portion of harmful data is removed, the ECIF method significantly outperforms random removal, particularly when the removal proportion is small.}
elements.sent-2411-440: Specifically, when less than 40\% of the data is removed, ECIF achieves an accuracy improvement of over 10\% compared to random removal.
elements.sent-2411-441: This demonstrates the capability of ECIF to accurately identify harmful samples, thereby substantially enhancing the model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-442: To provide a method as the reference, we adopt CLIPScore~\citep{hessel2021clipscore , a basic data evaluation method, as the baseline for MLLM.}
elements.sent-2411-443: This method is model-independent and is limited to evaluating data quality rather than assessing the contribution of the data to the model.

elements.sent-2411-444: In the task of harmful data removal, the ECIF method demonstrates significantly better performance compared to the CLIP Score.
elements.sent-2411-445: For valuable data removal, ECIF performs slightly better than the CLIP Score.
elements.sent-2411-446: This superiority is primarily attributed to ECIF's ability to attribute data based on the relationship between the model and the data, whereas CLIP Score is solely used to evaluate data quality without considering the model's involvement.
